fragments.content: Cancer staging is a standardized method to describe the extent of cancer in the body. The TNM system, developed by AJCC and UICC, classifies cancers by: T (Tumor) - size and extent of primary tumor (T0-T4); N (Nodes) - whether cancer has spread to nearby lymph nodes (N0-N3); M (Metastasis) - whether cancer has spread to distant organs (M0 or M1). Stage groups combine TNM into overall stages: Stage 0 (carcinoma in situ), Stage I (localized, small tumor), Stage II-III (regional spread, larger tumors or node involvement), Stage IV (distant metastasis). Staging determines prognosis, guides treatment selection, enables comparison of outcomes across institutions, and facilitates clinical trial enrollment. Anatomic staging uses imaging and pathology; prognostic stage groups incorporate biomarkers for certain cancers.

Treatment protocols are determined by cancer stage. Stage 0 (in situ): local excision or topical therapy, cure rates >95%. Stage I: surgery alone often curative; adjuvant therapy considered based on risk factors (e.g., breast cancer Stage I may receive radiation post-lumpectomy). Stage II: multimodal therapy - surgery plus adjuvant chemotherapy/radiation; neoadjuvant therapy increasingly used to downstage tumors. Stage III: aggressive multimodal treatment - neoadjuvant chemotherapy/radiation followed by surgery, or definitive chemoradiation; adjuvant therapy standard. Stage IV: systemic therapy primary (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy); palliative radiation/surgery for symptom control; oligometastatic disease may benefit from metastasectomy. Stage-directed trials show 5-year survival varies dramatically: Stage I breast cancer >99%, Stage IV <30%; Stage I colon cancer >90%, Stage IV <15%. Restaging after neoadjuvant therapy guides subsequent treatment.

Staging requires comprehensive diagnostic workup. Imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for most solid tumors; MRI for brain, rectal, prostate cancers; PET-CT for lymphoma, lung cancer, melanoma; bone scan for prostate, breast cancer with symptoms. Pathology: biopsy confirms diagnosis, provides histologic grade; surgical specimens determine pathologic stage (pTNM). Endoscopy: colonoscopy for colorectal cancer, bronchoscopy for lung cancer, EGD for GI cancers. Bone marrow biopsy for hematologic malignancies. Biomarkers: PSA for prostate, CA-125 for ovarian, CEA for colorectal monitoring. Molecular testing: EGFR/ALK for lung cancer, HER2 for breast, KRAS/NRAS for colorectal, BRAF for melanoma. Clinical stage (cTNM) based on pre-treatment evaluation; pathologic stage (pTNM) after surgery; restaging (rTNM) for recurrence. AJCC 8th edition incorporates molecular markers for breast, prostate, thyroid cancers into prognostic stage groups.
